entities.label: Frederick J. Furnivall's A Royal Historie of the Excellent Knight Generides (1865, with 1866 postscript)

The scanned copy represents an augmented physical state containing 1866 matter despite its 1865 title page; the exact issue history of that augmentation remains unresolved.

holding.notes: Furnivall edited the codex while it belonged to John Tollemache and was preserved at Helmingham Hall; the Morgan later purchased it from A. Rosenthal.

physicalDescription.format: bound codex; two columns; 60 lines per page through f. 152 and 52 lines on f. 153
notes: The codex contains an incomplete copy of Lydgate's Troy Book at ff. 1r-102v, Sir Generides at ff. 103r-152v, and a thirteenth-century Psalm-commentary leaf at f. 153r.
The Morgan records two painted miniatures and five sketched but unpainted miniatures; illumination is unfinished.
The current binding is eighteenth-century tan sprinkled calf with Tollemache-Dysart arms; the complete binding and endpapers are not exposed in the public seven-image selection.

reviewNotes: The intellectual subject is the anonymous late-fourteenth-century Middle English couplet version of Generides, not the independent stanzaic version, Morgan MS M.876 as a whole, Furnivall's edition, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML.
The manuscript work occupies ff. 103r-152v. Its prose contents rubric begins on f. 103r; Furnivall's first verse milestone places the prologue on f. 103v (his '103b').

The seven Morgan web images are an official selection, not a complete manuscript facsimile. Images 4-7 overlap the work at ff. 113r, 119v, 121v, and 133r; images 1-3 show adjacent Lydgate material at ff. 6r, 59r, and 87r.

Furnivall's title page is dated 1865, while the scanned volume also contains a postscript dated 19 February 1866 and a 1866 Roxburghe Club membership list. Those dates are not composition dates of the medieval romance.
Dalziel's working transcript is provisionally typed manuscript_witness because schema 1.0.0 has no historical editorial-transcript entity type; it is not a second medieval witness.

lineage.summary: The checked-in CME00043 XML is a repository copy of the University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse text derived from Frederick J. Furnivall's Roxburghe Club edition A Royal Historie of the Excellent Knight Generides. The encoding selects the edition's dedication leaf, the manuscript-derived prose summary, and the complete 10,086-line couplet romance on printed pp. 1-312; it omits the edition's title page, contents, preface, fragments of the independent stanzaic version, corrigenda, 1866 postscript, glossary, and index. Furnivall states that W. A. Dalziel made the working transcript from the sole continuous witness, now Morgan Library & Museum MS M.876, ff. 103r-152v, and that Furnivall checked the transcript and printed text. A complete scan of the augmented 1865/1866 printed volume is cached. The Morgan publicly exposes only seven selected 600-pixel manuscript images, four of them within Generides; that complete published selection is cached as partial manuscript coverage. No public complete-object facsimile of M.876 was found, but one can be requested for a fee through the Morgan's Imaging & Rights service.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Composed in the late fourteenth century; attested in a circa 1440-1450 manuscript; first printed in 1865
catalogSummary.title: Generides (couplet version)

catalogingSubject.label: Generides (couplet version)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the anonymous late-fourteenth-century Middle English couplet version of the romance Generides: a 10,086-line poem surviving continuously in Morgan Library & Museum MS M.876, ff. 103r-152v. It excludes the independent rhyme-royal or stanzaic version, its manuscripts and early printed fragments, the adjacent works and physical codex as a whole, Dalziel's editorial working transcript, Furnivall's 1865/1866 edition and paratexts, the U-M electronic encoding, the repository XML, and digital reproductions as distinct layers. The prose manuscript summary copied before the poem and Furnivall's modern marginal explanations are represented in CME00043 but are treated as witness and edition paratext rather than changing the romance's verse form.

contentParts.label: Prologue
contentParts.summary: The narrator praises the telling of old deeds and reports finding a tale associated with French rhyme and a Latin rendering at Hertford.

contentParts.label: Chapters I-XXII
contentParts.summary: The twenty-two chapter sequence narrates Generides's birth, exile, loves, wars, restorations, disguises, marriage, and succession.

assertion.notes: This is a work-level scholarly date, not the 1440-1450 production date of M.876.
This is the sole known continuous manuscript of the couplet version.

dateStatements.display: Attested in Morgan MS M.876, circa 1440-1450

assertion.notes: Furnivall's scan also contains a postscript dated 1866; that later paratext does not change the title-page date of the first couplet-text publication.

dateStatements.display: First printed in Furnivall's Roxburghe Club edition, 1865

extent.description: The encoded poem comprises a 32-line prologue and twenty-two numbered chapters containing 10,054 further lines, for 10,086 verse lines total in twenty-three LG elements. The count excludes the prose contents rubric, dedication, headings, and all editorial NOTE material; it agrees with the modern version-level bibliography's 10,086-line extent.

assertion.notes: The preceding prose contents rubric and modern editorial prose do not make the cataloged work mixed-form.

formStatements.description: A 10,086-line narrative poem in rhyming couplets, organized in Furnivall and the XML as a 32-line prologue plus twenty-two chapters.

assertion.notes: The narrative centers on knightly prowess, loyalty, warfare, courtly love, dynastic restoration, and marriage.
The prologue and older manuscript scholarship report a French source, possibly through Latin, but the exact source and direct route remain unidentified.
Modern English dedication, marginal explanations, preface, and apparatus belong to the edition and encoding layers, not to the abstract medieval romance's language facet.

notes: Work, version, manuscript witness, working transcript, scholarly edition, electronic encoding, repository artifact, catalog description, and facsimile are separate cataloging layers.
Morgan MS M.876 is the sole continuous witness of the couplet version; Trinity O.5.2 and Emmanuel 405 belong to the independent stanzaic version.
The 10,086-line total is both computationally reproducible from direct BODY L elements and independently reported by the current version-level bibliography.

A public seven-image manuscript selection does not support complete-codex claims; the lineage manifest records its four-image work overlap and the request route for a complete physical-object facsimile.

next_steps: Compare both full English versions and the Emmanuel fragments against current source scholarship and proposed analogues.
Distinguish common narrative ancestry, direct translation, adaptation, and independent translation rather than treating shared plot as proof of one route.
Search for evidence of the reported French and Latin texts without converting a prologue claim into a surviving-source entity.
openQuestions.question: What demonstrable textual relationship connects the couplet and stanzaic English versions, and what evidence survives for the prologue's reported French-to-Latin-to-English source chain?

next_steps: Consult current linguistic analysis of M.876 and distinguish authorial language from scribal copying language.
Check whether modern scholarship localizes the couplet version independently of the stanzaic version.
Do not infer composition at Hertford from the prologue's source-story or at Helmingham from later ownership.
openQuestions.question: How securely can the couplet version's Midlands composition be localized, and how does that claim relate to the mixed Midland and Northern features Furnivall observed in M.876?

assertion.notes: The modern database states Midlands. Furnivall cautiously describes the surviving copy's language as perhaps Midland with Northern mixture; witness dialect and work composition place are not assumed identical.
No named medieval author is established. Furnivall, Dalziel, Gibbs, and Tollemache belong to later editorial, transcription, presentation, or ownership layers.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous Middle English translator or adapter

assertion.notes: The prologue reports French verse rendered into Latin at Hertford before the English tale, and older cataloging treats the two English versions as independent translations from French. No exact French or Latin source text or physical exemplar has been identified, so translation is not modeled as a fully resolved direct-source chain.

responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous Middle English translator or adapter

notes: Furnivall established the printed reading text, editorial expansions, rubrication signals, bracketed supplies, notes, glossary, and index, and checked Dalziel's transcript and the printed text.

scope.description: Immediate 1865 printed edition of the couplet version

notes: The working transcript is a material intermediary in the edition's lineage, not the medieval work itself.

scope.description: Working transcript of the couplet text used for Furnivall's edition

subjects.label: Kingship, succession, conquest, and political legitimacy
False accusation, delayed execution, and rash judgment

scope.description: Sirenydes's false accusation against Generides and the Persian court's judgment after Malachias's death
Generides's disguised rescue of Clarionas and Clarionas's later disguised test and healing of Generides

summaries.value: Generides, the unacknowledged son of King Aufreus of India and Sereyne of Syria, enters his father's service, resists the adulterous queen Sirenydes, and leaves for Persia after the traitor Amalek turns her accusation against him. He wins distinction in war and the love of the sultan's daughter Clarionas, defeats rivals and invading kings, helps restore Aufreus, survives further betrayals and separations, and ultimately marries Clarionas and succeeds to kingdoms in India and Persia. The romance combines dynastic restoration, courtly love, battlefield prowess, disguise and recognition, loyal friendship, and providential prediction.

A 32-line prologue on the value and transmission of old tales precedes twenty-two chapters. The chapters narrate Aufreus and Sereyne's meeting and Generides's birth; Sirenydes and Amalek's betrayal; Generides and Clarionas's courtship; wars against Bellyns and Gwynan; Clarionas's abduction and rescue; the campaigns to restore India; Amalek's death and Sirenydes's repentance; Generides's grief, disguised reunion with Clarionas, marriage, succession, and rewards to loyal companions.

This record describes only the couplet version. The rhyme-royal stanzaic version survives in Trinity College Cambridge MS O.5.2 and fragments in Emmanuel College MS 405 and early print, and it must not be counted as a second witness to this exact 10,086-line couplet text. Furnivall printed samples of that other version in the same bound volume, but CME00043 does not encode them.

The XML's two front divisions are edition and manuscript paratext: a modern dedication leaf and a prose contents rubric. The poem itself is the 10,086 L elements in the twenty-three BODY/DIV1 verse groups. Furnivall's marginal modern-English explanations and textual notes are retained at the encoding layer but are not counted as medieval verse or used to make the work multilingual or mixed-form.

assertion.notes: The parenthetical qualifier distinguishes this text from the independent stanzaic version without treating either manuscript or edition title as the abstract work's only name.

titles.supplied_by: General Corpus from modern version-level bibliography

assertion.notes: This conventional title can refer broadly to both long Middle English versions; use the preferred qualified title when version precision matters.
The complete edition also contains an editor's preface, fragments of the stanzaic version, glossary, index, and a later postscript; those are not part of the cataloged romance.

scope.description: Bibliographic title of the immediate edition and a conventional long title for its couplet text

titles.value: A Royal Historie of the Excellent Knight Generides
A man that hath litel to doone, Werk he may make him soone
Eternallie there to be! Seith Amen al, for charitie.

assertion.notes: The full colophon continues with Generides's imperial and hereditary titles; bracketed words in the edition reflect manuscript damage.

titles.supplied_by: Morgan MS M.876 colophon, with damaged words supplied in Furnivall's edition
titles.value: Here endeth the noble historie of the excellent Prince Generides

work.author: Anonymous
work.dateDisplay: Composed in the late fourteenth century; attested in a circa 1440-1450 manuscript; first printed in 1865

publication.embeddedTitle: TO THE PRESIDENT AND MEMBERS OF The Roxburghe Club

subjects.label: Kingship, succession, conquest, and political legitimacy
False accusation, delayed execution, and rash judgment

work.summary: Generides, the unacknowledged son of King Aufreus of India and Sereyne of Syria, enters his father's service, resists the adulterous queen Sirenydes, and leaves for Persia after the traitor Amalek turns her accusation against him. He wins distinction in war and the love of the sultan's daughter Clarionas, defeats rivals and invading kings, helps restore Aufreus, survives further betrayals and separations, and ultimately marries Clarionas and succeeds to kingdoms in India and Persia. The romance combines dynastic restoration, courtly love, battlefield prowess, disguise and recognition, loyal friendship, and providential prediction.
